Effective And Honest Scales

Goal

This notebook shows how scale choices affect interpretation.

Analytic Questions

  • How can axis limits exaggerate or reduce apparent differences?
  • When should a scale start at zero?
  • How do we show a focused comparison without misleading the reader?

Load Data

flu <- readr::read_csv("data/real/flu_vaccination_age_time_clean.csv")
Rows: 9811 Columns: 11
── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Delimiter: ","
chr (4): season, geography_type, geography, age_group
dbl (7): season_start_year, month, fips, estimate, ci_lower, ci_upper, sampl...

ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data.
ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message.

Prepare Data

Data preparation step Functions in this chunk
Keep or remove rows filter() 
us_may <- flu |>
  filter(
    geography == "United States",
    month == 5,
    age_group %in% c("18-49 Years", "50-64 Years", ">=65 Years"),
    !is.na(estimate)
  )

Focusing In

coord_cartesian() zooms the displayed y-axis without removing rows from the data.

Plot component Code in this chunk
Aesthetic mappings x; y; color
Geoms / stats geom_line(); geom_point()
geom_line() properties linewidth
geom_point() properties size
Scales / coordinates / facets / labels coord_cartesian(); labs(); theme_minimal() 
p1 <- ggplot(us_may, aes(x = season_start_year, y = estimate, color = age_group)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  coord_cartesian(ylim = c(25, 80))

p2 <- p1 +
  labs(
    title = "Focused Axis For Comparing Seasonal Changes",
    subtitle = "United States, May (month 5)",
    x = "Season start year",
    y = "Vaccination estimate (%)",
    color = "Age group"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

p2

The focused axis makes year-to-year changes easier to see. That being said, zooming to “frame” the data is not always desirable. There are times when it is useful to contain certain values which may not be contained in the data. Use your judgment.

Framing Based Upon A Benchmark

p1 <- ggplot(us_may, aes(x = season_start_year, y = estimate, color = age_group)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  coord_cartesian(ylim = c(0, 100))

p2 <- p1 +
  labs(
    title = "Full Percent Scale Shows The Size Of The Estimates",
    subtitle = "United States, May (month 5)",
    x = "Season start year",
    y = "Vaccination estimate (%)",
    color = "Age group"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

p2

The full 100% scale makes the absolute level clearer, but compresses small changes. I think this is an example where keeping the whole range of possible y-axis values is superior to zooming in.

Same Data, Different Scale

The same data can look very different when the y-axis changes. This example uses a rare adverse event: 0.001% in standard care and 0.003% in a new treatment group.

The relative difference is real: the event is three times as common in the new treatment group. But the absolute risk is still very small.

Data preparation step Functions in this chunk
Create or change columns mutate() 
adverse_event_risk <- tibble::tibble(
  treatment = c("Standard care", "New treatment"),
  risk = c(0.00001, 0.00003)
) |>
  mutate(
    treatment = factor(treatment, levels = treatment),
    risk_label = scales::percent(risk, accuracy = 0.001)
  )

Tight Scale

This plot is not strictly speaking inaccurate, but it is misleading. It makes a tiny absolute risk look visually large because the y-axis only covers a very small range.

Plot component Code in this chunk
Aesthetic mappings x; y; label
Geoms / stats geom_col(); geom_text()
geom_col() properties fill; width
geom_text() properties size
Scales / coordinates / facets / labels scale_y_continuous(); labs(); theme_minimal() 
ggplot(adverse_event_risk, aes(x = treatment, y = risk)) +
  geom_col(fill = "steelblue", width = 0.65) +
  geom_text(aes(label = risk_label), vjust = -0.5, size = 3.5) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    limits = c(0, 0.00004),
    labels = scales::label_percent(accuracy = 0.001)
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "A Tight Scale Emphasizes The Relative Difference",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Risk of adverse event"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

Full Probability Scale

The same data on a full probability scale tells a different story. The relative difference still exists, but the absolute risk is tiny.

ggplot(adverse_event_risk, aes(x = treatment, y = risk)) +
  geom_col(fill = "steelblue", width = 0.65) +
  geom_text(aes(label = risk_label), vjust = -0.5, size = 3.5) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    limits = c(0, 1),
    breaks = seq(0, 1, 0.2),
    labels = scales::label_percent(accuracy = 1)
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "A Full Scale Shows The Absolute Risk",
    x = NULL,
    y = "Risk of adverse event"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

Neither plot changes the data. The question is what readers need to understand: the relative increase, the absolute risk, or both.

Bars Usually Need Zero

Data preparation step Functions in this chunk
Keep or remove rows filter()
Create or change columns mutate()
Plot component Code in this chunk
Aesthetic mappings x; y
Geoms / stats geom_col()
geom_col() properties fill
Scales / coordinates / facets / labels coord_flip(); labs(); theme_minimal() 
latest <- us_may |>
  filter(season_start_year == max(season_start_year)) |>
  mutate(age_group = reorder(age_group, estimate))

p1 <- ggplot(latest, aes(x = age_group, y = estimate)) +
  geom_col(fill = "steelblue") +
  coord_flip(ylim = c(0, 100))

p2 <- p1 +
  labs(
    title = "Bars Encode Length From A Baseline",
    subtitle = "Latest available season, United States, May (month 5)",
    x = "Age group",
    y = "Vaccination estimate (%)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

p2

Takeaways

  • For line plots, a narrowed y-axis can help compare change, but the title, subtitle, or caption should make the axis range obvious.
  • Percent scales often need context for the full 0-100 range.
  • Tight scales can emphasize relative differences, but readers may also need absolute-risk context.
  • Bars encode length from a baseline, so zero usually matters.
  • Do an honesty gut-check: is this scale the most honest way to convey the data/findings?
